items.q: Can the fieldwork report be written entirely from internet sources?
items.correct: No - the internal assessment centres on primary data you collect in the field, although secondary sources can add context and comparison.
items.misconception: Good online research can replace collecting your own field data.
items.random_wrong: Secondary data means data collected in the afternoon.
items.why: The IA criteria assess your own information collection and processing; secondary material only supports interpretation.

items.q: Is an anomaly in your results a mistake you should delete?
items.correct: Not necessarily - anomalies may be genuine and worth explaining; data should only be discarded with a clear methodological justification.
items.misconception: All anomalous points are errors and should be removed before analysis.
items.random_wrong: A single anomaly makes the whole report fail.
items.why: Discussing anomalies honestly demonstrates critical thinking and often reveals real geographical processes at work.

items.q: Does a correlation found in your field data prove causation?
items.correct: No - your analysis can show association, but claiming causation requires supporting theory and acknowledgement of other possible factors.
items.misconception: If two variables I measured rise together, one must cause the other.
items.random_wrong: Correlation means the data were collected correctly.
items.why: Linking statistical results back to geographic theory, with caution about causality, is what earns evaluation marks.

items.q: At the 0.05 significance level, what does a significant result mean?
items.correct: There is less than a 5% probability the pattern arose by chance
items.why: It's There is less than a 5% probability the pattern arose by chance. “The result is 95% wrong” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which measure describes the spread of data around the mean?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Standard deviation. “Mode” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Random, systematic and stratified are types of what?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sampling strategy. “Statistical test” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why pilot your data-collection methods before the main survey?
items.correct: To find problems and improve reliability before committing time
items.misconception: To obtain the final results without checks
items.random_wrong: Because drones are compulsory in fieldwork
items.why: The answer examiners want is: To find problems and improve reliability before committing time. “To obtain the final results without checks” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Common significance level for hypothesis testing?
items.why: The real figure is 0.05 p. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
